NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 17 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, the arbitral tribunal has the power to grant interim measures, which include:

1. Appointment of a guardian for a minor or person of unsound mind (Section 17(1)(a))
2. Preservation, interim custody or sale of any goods which are the subject-matter of the arbitration agreement (Section 17(1)(b))
3. Securing the amount in dispute in the arbitration (Section 17(1)(c))
4. Detention, preservation or inspection of any property or thing which is the subject-matter of the dispute (Section 17(1)(d))
5. Interim injunction or the appointment of a receiver (Section 17(1)(e))

These interim measures are subject to the conditions and procedures specified in Section 17(2) and (3) of the Act.
citations: the Arbitration and Conciliation Act Section 17
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award can be set aside on the following grounds:

1. A party was under some incapacity (Section 34(2)(a)).
2. The arbitration agreement is not valid under the law to which the parties have subjected it or, failing any indication thereon, under the law of India (Section 34(2)(a)).
3. The party making the application was not given proper notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the arbitral proceedings or was otherwise unable to present his case (Section 34(2)(a)).
4. The arbitral award deals with a dispute not contemplated by or not falling within the terms of the submission to arbitration (Section 34(2)(a)).
5. The composition of the arbitral tribunal or the arbitral procedure was not in accordance with the agreement of the parties (Section 34(2)(a)).
6. The subject-matter of the dispute is not capable of settlement by arbitration under the law of India (Section 34(2)(b)).
7. The arbitral award is in conflict with the public policy of India (Section 34(2)(b)).

These grounds are specified in Section 34(2) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
citations: the Arbitration and Conciliation Act Section 34(2)
the Arbitration and Conciliation Act Section 34
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer: Under Section 36 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award becomes enforceable only after the time for making an application to set aside the award under Section 34 has expired, or if such an application has been made, after the application has been disposed of by the court. (Section 36, Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996).
citations: the Arbitration and Conciliation Act Section 36

NoRAG.answer:
The essential ingredients of an offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, are:

1. There must be a cheque (Section 6 of the NI Act).
2. The cheque must have been drawn by the accused for the discharge of a debt or liability (Section 138(a) of the NI Act).
3. The cheque is presented to the bank within the period of its validity (Section 138(b) of the NI Act).
4. The cheque is returned by the bank unpaid, either because of insufficient funds or because the amount exceeds the arrangement made with the bank (Section 138(b) of the NI Act).
5. The drawer (accused) is given notice of the dishonour of the cheque within 30 days from the date of receipt of the information by the holder of the cheque regarding the return of the cheque as unpaid (Section 138(b) of the NI Act, read with Section 138(c) of the NI Act, and Clause (c) of the Proviso to Section 138).
6. The drawer (accused) fails to make the payment of the debt or other liability to the payee or the holder of the cheque within 15 days of the receipt of the notice (Section 138(c) of the NI Act, read with Clause (c) of the Proviso to Section 138).

These ingredients must be fulfilled to constitute an offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881.
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer: Section 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, creates a presumption that the cheque was issued in discharge of a debt or liability. This presumption can be rebutted by the accused. (Section 139, Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881)
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 140 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, the following defences are barred:

1. Defence of a lack of consideration (Section 140).
2. Defence that the instrument was completed unless the completion was fraudulent (Section 140).

This means that in a case where a cheque is dishonoured, the drawer cannot claim that the cheque was not supported by consideration or that it was incomplete at the time of delivery, unless the completion was done fraudulently.
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 141 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, vicarious liability is fixed for companies as follows: 

If a person commits an offence under Section 138 (dishonour of cheque) and the offence is attributed to a company, every person who, at the time the offence was committed, was in charge of and was responsible to the company for the conduct of the business of the company, as well as the company, shall be deemed to be guilty of the offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly.

This section imposes vicarious liability on the company and its officers in charge of and responsible for the conduct of the business. 

(Section 141, Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881)
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 142 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, a court can take cognizance of an offence if the following conditions are met:

1. The complaint is made in writing by the payee or the holder of the cheque.
2. The complaint is made within one month of the date on which the cause of action arises (Section 142(b) of the NI Act).
3. The complaint is made before a court within whose local jurisdiction the offence was committed (Section 142(c) of the NI Act).

Reference: Section 142 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881.
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Section 143 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (NI Act) provides for summary trial in cases of cheque dishonour. According to this section, if a magistrate is satisfied that there is sufficient material for proceeding, the case can be tried summarily. 

In a summary trial under Section 143, the procedure is as follows:
1. The Magistrate shall issue a summons to the accused.
2. Upon the accused's appearance, the Magistrate shall hear the parties and try the case.
3. The Magistrate may, after considering the evidence, record a judgment of conviction or acquittal.

The summary trial procedure aims to expedite the disposal of cheque dishonour cases. However, if the Magistrate feels that the case is not fit for summary trial, they may proceed with the regular trial procedure as per the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (CrPC).
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer: Under Section 143A of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, interim compensation can be awarded during the trial of a cheque dishonour case. It can be awarded by the court if it is of the opinion that there is a prima facie case against the accused. The interim compensation shall not exceed 20% of the cheque amount. (Section 143A, Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881)
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 145 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, evidence by affidavit is treated as a valid mode of proof. Section 145 states that "the court may presume that the negotiable instrument was drawn or made for consideration, and that every person who drew, made, accepted, endorsed or transferred it had authority to do so, and that it was drawn, made, accepted, endorsed or transferred for a genuine transaction." 

However, the proviso to Section 145 allows the court to accept affidavit evidence in certain circumstances. It states that "the court may, in its discretion, require the person to produce the instrument or to give evidence orally, if it is of opinion that it is necessary to justice." 

In practice, courts in India have held that affidavit evidence can be used to prove certain facts under Section 145, but the court has the discretion to require oral evidence if it deems necessary (K. Bhaskaran v. S. Padmanabhan, (1999) 2 SCC 695).
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer: Yes, compounding is permitted under Section 147 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. Section 147 states that "every offence punishable under this Act may be compounded by the person injured by the offence, and, where the offence was committed by a company, or an association of persons, whether incorporated or not, every director, manager, or secretary, or other officer of the company, or association, may compound such offence on behalf of the company or association, as the case may be, with the permission of the Court, and the provisions of Section 320 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974), shall, as far as may be, apply to such compounding."
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 148 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, the appellate court has the power to order payment of interest on the amount of the decree or order appealed against, from the date of the decree or order, until payment. 

Section 148 states: "On an application made to it in this behalf, the Appellate Court may order the appellant to pay interest at such rate as the Court thinks reasonable on the amount with which the judgment or order appealed against is made, from the date of the decree or order until payment."

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 5 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), a valid Hindu marriage requires the following conditions:

1. Monogamy: Neither party has a spouse living at the time of marriage (Section 5(i)).
2. Mental capacity: Both parties must be mentally sound and capable of giving valid consent (Section 5(ii)).
3. Age: The bride must be at least 18 years old, and the groom must be at least 21 years old (Section 5(iii)).
4. Sapinda relationship: The parties must not be sapindas (related by blood or marriage) unless permitted by custom or usage (Section 5(iv)).
5. Degrees of prohibited relationship: The parties must not be within the degrees of prohibited relationship unless permitted by custom or usage (Section 5(v)).
6. Other conditions: The marriage must be performed in accordance with the customs and rituals of the parties, and the parties must not be from different castes or communities that prohibit inter-caste or inter-community marriages, unless permitted by custom or usage.

These conditions are essential for a valid Hindu marriage under the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955.
NoRAG.disclaimer: Response generated from LLM training data without retrieval. May not reflect recent law changes.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 7 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), the following ceremonies are recognized for the solemnization of a Hindu marriage:

1. Brahma marriage
2. Daiva marriage

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 8 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), registration of a Hindu marriage is not mandatory but optional. The legal effect of registration under Section 8 HMA is that it provides conclusive proof of the marriage, making it easier to establish the validity of the marriage in case of disputes or legal proceedings.

Registration under Section 8 HMA serves as prima facie evidence of the marriage, and it can be used to prove the marriage in court. However, it does not affect the validity of the marriage itself, as a Hindu marriage is considered valid if it is performed in accordance with the customs and rituals of the parties involved, as per Section 5 and 7 of the HMA.

In other words, registration under Section 8 HMA is a procedural formality that provides documentary evidence of the marriage, but it is not a condition precedent for the validity of the marriage.

NoRAG.answer: Under Section 9 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), a petition for restitution of conjugal rights can be filed when a spouse has withdrawn from the society of the other spouse without a reasonable cause. The court may decree restitution of conjugal rights if it is satisfied that the petitioner has a legitimate grievance and the respondent has withdrawn from the society of the petitioner without a reasonable cause (Section 9, HMA).

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 10 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), judicial separation is a legal proceeding where a court orders the separation of a married couple. This section allows either spouse to petition for judicial separation on certain grounds, such as cruelty, desertion, or adultery, among others.

During a judicial separation, the couple is required to live apart, but the marriage is not dissolved. The court may also make interim orders regarding maintenance, custody of children, and other related matters.

Section 10 of the HMA states: "Judicial separation.- (1) Either party to a marriage may, without alleging any fault on the part of the other party, present a petition to the district court for an order for judicial separation on the ground that they have been living separately for a period of one year or more..."

Under Section 12 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), a marriage is voidable on the following grounds:

1. Impotency of the respondent at the time of marriage (Section 12(1)(a) HMA)
2. Respondent's unsound mind or mental disorder, making them unfit for marriage (Section 12(1)(b) HMA)
3. Respondent suffering from a virulent and incurable form of leprosy (Section 12(1)(c) HMA)
4. Respondent suffering from a venereal disease in a communicable form (Section 12(1)(d) HMA)
5. Pregnancy of the respondent by some other person at the time of marriage (Section 12(1)(e) HMA)
6. Consent obtained by force, coercion, or fraud (Section 12(1)(c) read with Section 12(2) HMA, in conjunction with Section 14 HMA, but primarily under Section 14 is not a ground for voidable marriage, however, Section 12(2) provides that if the consent was obtained by force or fraud, the marriage can be avoided, if the petition is presented within a reasonable time of the forced or fraudulent marriage)
7. Consent not given due to a mistake as to the identity of the person or the nature of the ceremony (Section 12(2) HMA, in conjunction with the principles of Section 14 HMA).

These grounds must be proven to the satisfaction of the court for the marriage to be declared voidable under Section 12 HMA.

Under Section 13 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), the grounds for divorce are as follows:

1. Adultery (Section 13(1)(i))
2. Cruelty (Section 13(1)(ia))
3. Desertion for at least 2 years (Section 13(1)(ib))
4. Conversion to another religion (Section 13(1)(ii))
5. Mental disorder (Section 13(1)(iii))
6. Leprosy (Section 13(1)(iv))
7. Venereal disease (Section 13(1)(v))
8. Renunciation of the world (Section 13(1)(vi))
9. Presumption of death (Section 13(1)(vii))
10. Non-resumption of cohabitation after a decree of judicial separation (Section 13(1)(viii))
11. Non-compliance with a decree of restitution of conjugal rights (Section 13(1)(ix))

These grounds are applicable to both husband and wife.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 13B of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), a mutual consent divorce can be granted if the following statutory conditions are met:

1. The petition must be presented jointly by both parties (Section 13B(1)).
2. The parties must have been living separately for a period of at least one year (Section 13B(1)(i)).
3. The parties must have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved (Section 13B(1)(ii)).
4. The petition must state that the parties have not been able to live together and have mutually agreed to dissolve the marriage (Section 13B(2)).
5. After the petition is presented, a period of six months must elapse, but not more than 18 months, before the divorce can be granted (Section 13B(2)).
6. During this period, the parties must not have withdrawn their consent to the divorce (Section 13B(2)).

If these conditions are met, the court may grant a decree of divorce by mutual consent under Section 13B of the HMA.

NoRAG.answer:
Under Section 25 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (HMA), permanent alimony is dealt with as follows: 

If the court is satisfied that a spouse has not sufficient independent income for their support and the necessary expenses of the proceeding, it may order the other spouse to pay permanent alimony. 

Section 25(1) states: "Where a marriage is dissolved by a decree of divorce, and the wife is not adulterous, and is not guilty of desertion, the court may, on application made to it by the wife, order the husband to pay to her such gross sum or such monthly or periodical sum for a term not exceeding her life, as, having regard to her own income, and other property, if any, and to the income and other property of the husband, it thinks reasonable."

Section 25(2) further states: "If the husband has transferred, after the solemnization of the marriage, any property to his wife, or has entered into any agreement (whether before or after the marriage), to pay to her a sum for her maintenance, such transfer or agreement shall not be taken into account in deciding the amount of maintenance which the husband shall be ordered to pay under this section."

NoRAG.answer: Under Section 2(a) of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005, an "aggrieved person" means any woman who is, or has been, in a domestic relationship with the respondent and who alleges to have been subjected to any act of domestic violence by the respondent.

An application to a Magistrate under Section 12 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005, is made by the aggrieved person or any person on her behalf. The application can be made to the Magistrate in the form of an affidavit, as per Section 12(1) of the Act.

The application should be filed before the Magistrate having jurisdiction in the area where the domestic violence has taken place or where the respondent resides or works, as per Section 27 of the Act.

The application should contain the details of the domestic violence, the relief sought, and any other relevant information. The Magistrate may also grant interim relief to the aggrieved person under Section 23 of the Act, pending the disposal of the application.

NoRAG.answer: Under Section 17 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005, a woman has the right to reside in the shared household, whether or not she has any title or rights in the property. Under Section 18 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005, the following protection orders can be passed:

1. Restraining the respondent from alienating any assets, operating bank lockers or bank accounts used or enjoyed by both parties, etc. (Section 18(a))
2. Directing the respondent to remove himself from the shared household (Section 18(b))
3. Restraining the respondent or his relatives from entering the workplace or any other place frequented by the aggrieved person (Section 18(c))
4. Protecting the aggrieved person from domestic violence (Section 18(d))
5. Restraining the respondent from causing violence to the aggrieved person, her relatives, or any other person (Section 18(e))
6. Restraining the respondent from aiding or abetting the commission of acts of domestic violence (Section 18(f))
7. Directing the respondent to return the stridhan (gifts given to the woman at the time of marriage) or any other property or valuable security to the aggrieved person (Section 18(g))
8. Directing the respondent to pay monetary relief to the aggrieved person (Section 18(h))
9. Directing the respondent to provide alternative accommodation to the aggrieved person (Section 18(i))
10. Any other order as the Magistrate considers necessary and reasonable (Section 18(j)).

These orders are aimed at protecting the aggrieved person from domestic violence and providing her with a safe and secure environment.

Under Section 23 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005, a Magistrate may pass interim and ex parte orders to protect the aggrieved person. These orders may include:

Under Section 3 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (POCSO), penetrative sexual assault is defined as:

"...whoever, commits penetration of any of the following:

(b) any other object into the vagina, mouth, urethra or anus of the child; or 
(c) any part of the body of the accused into the vagina, mouth, urethra or anus of the child; or 
(d) makes the child do so with him or any other person,

Under Section 5 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, penetrative sexual assault becomes aggravated in certain circumstances. 

According to Section 5 (m) of the POCSO Act, penetrative sexual assault is considered aggravated when it is committed by a person in a position of authority or trust, or by a person who is a member of the security forces or police, or when the child is mentally ill or has a physical disability, among other circumstances.

Specifically, Section 5 (m) states that penetrative sexual assault is aggravated when it is committed:

- by a person in authority (Section 5(m)(i))
- by a person in a position of trust or authority towards the child (Section 5(m)(ii))
- by a person who is a member of the security forces or police (Section 5(m)(iii))
- by a person who is known to the child or is a family member (Section 5(m)(iv))
- when the child is mentally ill or has a physical disability (Section 5(m)(v))
- when the offender is suffering from HIV or any other life-threatening disease (Section 5(m)(vi))
- when the offender is a previous convict for any offence under this Act (Section 5(m)(vii))
- when the child is below the age of 12 years (Section 5(m)(viii))
- when the offender makes the child pregnant (Section 5(m)(ix))
- when the offender inflicts severe physical harm to the child (Section 5(m)(x))
- when the offender uses deadly weapons or causes grievous hurt (Section 5(m)(xi))
- when the offender makes the child bare or exhibit her private parts (Section 5(m)(xii))

Based on the provided context, the sentence for drunk driving causing death can be determined by referring to Section 304A of the Indian Penal Code 1860. 

According to Section 304A, whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

However, it is essential to note that Section 85 provides a defense for acts done by a person incapable of judgment due to intoxication caused against his will. But if the intoxication was self-induced, this defense would not be applicable.

In the case of drunk driving, if the driver was intoxicated voluntarily, Section 85 would not apply, and the driver could be held liable under Section 304A for causing death by negligence. The sentence would be imprisonment for up to two years, or a fine, or both.

On the other hand, if the driver was intoxicated against his will (as per Section 85), he might not be held liable for the offence. But this would depend on the specific circumstances of the case.

Additionally, Section 279 under Chapter 14 also deals with rash or negligent driving, but it does not specifically address the issue of drunk driving causing death. It prescribes a punishment of imprisonment for up to six months, or a fine of up to one thousand rupees, or both, for rash or negligent driving that endangers human life or is likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person. 

In summary, the sentence for drunk driving causing death would be imprisonment for up to two years, or a fine, or both, under Section 304A of the Indian Penal Code 1860, provided that the intoxication was self-induced and not administered against the driver's will.

The new bail provisions under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 (BNSS 2023) are as follows:

1. **Direction for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest**: Under Section 483 of BNSS 2023, a person who has reason to believe that they may be arrested on an accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence can apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction. The Court may, if it thinks fit, direct that in the event of such arrest, the person shall be released on bail. (Section 483, BNSS 2023, which replaces Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973)

2. **Special powers of High Court or Court of Session regarding bail**: Under Section 483 of BNSS 2023, a High Court or Court of Session has the power to:
   - Direct the release of a person accused of an offence and in custody on bail, and impose conditions if the offence is of a specific nature (Section 483(1)(a), BNSS 2023).
   - Set aside or modify any condition imposed by a Magistrate when releasing a person on bail (Section 483(1)(b), BNSS 2023).

These provisions aim to provide a framework for granting bail to individuals who are accused of non-bailable offences, while also giving the High Court and Court of Session special powers to impose conditions and modify bail orders.

SimpleRAG.answer:
The new provisions for anticipatory bail are outlined in Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023, which replaces Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973. According to this section, a person who has reason to believe that they may be arrested on an accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction under this section. The court may, if it thinks fit, direct that in the event of such arrest, the person shall be released on bail.

The guidelines for granting anticipatory bail, as laid down by the Supreme Court, are:

1. Consider the nature and gravity of the accusation (Source 1, case_law)
2. Examine the antecedents of the applicant
3. Assess whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant
4. Evaluate the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice

While granting bail, the court may impose conditions on the accused to ensure their appearance in court, such as requiring a bail bond and security deposit, directing periodic appearances before the police or court, surrendering their passport, avoiding contact with prosecution witnesses, and regular reporting to the police station (Source 2, case_law).
SimpleRAG.disclaimer: Response based on retrieved context. Does not check for overruled cases or recent amendments.

According to the Indian Penal Code (IPC) 1860, Section 411, which pertains to "Dishonestly receiving stolen property" (Bengali: বেঈমানিসহকারে চুরি করা সম্পত্তি গ্রহণ), states that whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

To understand what constitutes "stolen property", we can refer to Section 410, which defines it as property that has been transferred by theft, extortion, or robbery, or has been criminally misappropriated, or in respect of which a criminal breach of trust has been committed. However, if such property comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to it, it ceases to be stolen property (Source 2).

The definition of "theft" itself is provided in Section 378, which states that theft involves the dishonest intention to take movable property out of someone's possession without their consent (Source 3). 

Therefore, IPC 411 in BNS refers to the offense of dishonestly receiving or retaining property that has been stolen, with the knowledge or reason to believe that it is stolen, and is punishable under the Indian Penal Code.

According to the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, voluntarily causing grievous hurt is indeed different and is outlined in Section 117. This section states that whoever voluntarily causes hurt, with the intention or knowledge that it is likely to cause grievous hurt, and actually causes grievous hurt, is said to "voluntarily cause grievous hurt". 

As explained in Section 117, a person is not said to voluntarily cause grievous hurt unless they both cause grievous hurt and intend or know themselves to be likely to cause grievous hurt. However, if they intend or know themselves to be likely to cause grievous hurt of one kind but actually cause grievous hurt of another kind, they are still said to voluntarily cause grievous hurt.

The types of injuries that are considered "grievous" are outlined in Section 116, which includes emasculation, permanent privation of sight or hearing, privation of any member or joint, destruction or permanent impairing of the powers of any member or joint, permanent disfiguration of the head or face, fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth, and any hurt that endangers life or causes severe bodily pain or inability to follow ordinary pursuits for fifteen days.

In contrast to 'hurt', which refers to causing bodily pain, disease, or infirmity, 'grievous hurt' involves more severe injuries as outlined in Section 116. Therefore, voluntarily causing grievous hurt, as defined in Section 117, is a specific offense that requires both the intention or knowledge of causing grievous hurt and the actual causation of such hurt.

Section 204 of the BNS states that whoever secrets or destroys any document or electronic record that he may be lawfully compelled to produce as evidence in a Court of Justice, or in any proceeding lawfully held before a public servant, and does so with the intention of preventing its production or use as evidence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term that may extend to two years, or with a fine, or with both.

Therefore, the equivalent provision in BNS for IPC 201 (destruction of evidence) can be considered as Section 204 of the BNS.

The Supreme Court judgment on anticipatory bail principles is outlined in the context provided. According to the landmark judgment mentioned in Source 1 (case_law), the guidelines for granting anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC (now Section 483 BNSS) are:

1. Consider the nature and gravity of the accusation.
2. Examine the antecedents of the applicant.
3. Assess whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant.
4. Evaluate the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice.

The court emphasized that personal liberty is paramount and anticipatory bail should not be denied merely on the gravity of the accusation. This judgment highlights the importance of protecting personal liberty, as also reflected in Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, which is related to the right to life and personal liberty, as seen in the context of Source 3 (overruling_map) referencing Justice Bhagwati's views on speedy trial as a fundamental right.

The landmark judgment on rape and consent, as per the provided context, emphasizes that a mere breach of promise to marry does not automatically constitute rape under Section 376 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC). For a breach of promise to be considered rape, there must be evidence that the consent was obtained on a false promise made with mala fide intention from the inception, as clarified by the Supreme Court (Source 1).

This judgment highlights the importance of intent and the timing of the promise in determining whether a breach of promise to marry constitutes rape. It suggests that the court will examine whether the promise was false at the time it was made, and whether the accused had a mala fide intention from the beginning.

In relation to this, Section 375 of the IPC defines 'rape' as various acts involving penetration or manipulation without a woman's consent, considering factors like age, consent, mental or physical disability, and pregnancy (Source 3). Section 376 outlines the punishment for rape, ranging from ten years to life imprisonment, with a fine, and covers various aggravating factors (Source 2).

Therefore, the landmark judgment on rape and consent emphasizes the need for evidence of a false promise made with mala fide intention from the inception to constitute rape under Section 376 IPC.

The Supreme Court has provided guidelines on the death penalty, as seen in the case of Machhi Singh, which modified the Bachan Singh doctrine. According to these guidelines, the death penalty should be applied in the "rarest of rare" cases, which fall under five categories. This implies that the Supreme Court has established a framework for the application of the death penalty, ensuring that it is not arbitrarily or disproportionately applied.

As stated in Source 1 (case_law), an argument against capital punishment can be based on the constitutional right to life under Article 21, which guarantees the 'right to live with human dignity'. This section ensures that the accused has a fair opportunity to appeal the sentence, which is in line with the principles of natural justice and the right to a fair trial.

In conclusion, the Supreme Court's guidelines on the death penalty, as seen in the Machhi Singh case, provide a framework for the application of the death penalty, ensuring that it is applied in a fair and non-arbitrary manner. Based on the context provided, the determination of age in cases involving children is crucial in Indian law, particularly in the context of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, and the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000 (or its 2015 amendment).

1. **Procedure for Determining Age**: According to Section 34(2) of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, if any question arises in a proceeding before the Special Court regarding whether a person is a child or not, the Special Court shall determine this after satisfying itself about the age of such person. The court must record in writing its reasons for such determination.

2. **Applicability of Laws to Juveniles/Children**: The Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023, Section 300, explicitly states that nothing in that Chapter applies to any juvenile or child as defined in Section 2 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015. This implies that children are dealt with under special laws designed for their protection and care, rather than the general criminal law.

3. **Immature Understanding and Age**: Section 83 of the Indian Penal Code (as referenced in the case law) provides that nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven and under twelve years of age, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion. This section essentially recognizes that children under a certain age may not have the maturity to understand the implications of their actions and thus should not be held criminally liable in the same way as adults.

4. **Juvenile Justice Act**: The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000 (and its amendments), plays a significant role in defining who is considered a child and how they should be dealt with in the legal system. According to Section 34(1) of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, any offence under this Act committed by a child is to be dealt with under the provisions of the Juvenile Justice Act.

In conclusion, the determination of age and the handling of cases involving children are governed by specific laws and provisions in India, including the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000 (or 2015), and relevant sections of the Indian Penal Code like Section 83. These laws aim to protect children and ensure that they are dealt with appropriately, considering their age and understanding.

The Supreme Court's extraordinary powers under Article 142 of the Indian Constitution grant it the authority to pass any order necessary for doing complete justice in any cause or matter pending before it. As per Article 142(2), the Supreme Court has all and every power to make any order for the purpose of securing the attendance of any person, the discovery or production of any documents, or the investigation or punishment of any contempt of itself. This provision enables the Court to take measures that may not be explicitly provided for in other laws, but are necessary to ensure that justice is served. 

In addition to the powers under Article 142, the Supreme Court's role as a court of record under Article 129 and the supplemental powers that can be conferred by Parliament under Article 140 further reinforce its ability to effectively exercise its jurisdiction and enforce its rulings. 

Therefore, the Supreme Court's extraordinary powers under Article 142 are a key component of its ability to deliver complete justice and uphold the rule of law in India.

Based on the provided context, the following cases relate to compounding of offenses:

1. **Category B Offences**: These cannot be compounded if they are not considered 'First Offence' as defined in Source 2. A 'First Offence' refers to offenses committed prior to the initiation of prosecution or voluntarily disclosed by the assessee.

2. **Category A Offences**: These cannot be compounded if an application for compounding has already been made more than 3 times in respect of such offenses, unless approved by the Principal CCIT in exceptional circumstances (Source 3).

3. **General Compounding**: Compounding of an offense is a mechanism where a defaulter can pay a sum of money to escape prosecution, applicable to specified offenses that can be compounded by the competent authority either before or after the initiation of proceedings (Source 1).

These cases highlight the conditions and limitations under which offenses can or cannot be compounded, providing a framework for understanding the compounding of offenses within the given context.

The Supreme Court has clarified the scope of anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC, emphasizing that it is a valuable safeguard for personal liberty. For economic offenses, the court considers the following factors for granting anticipatory bail:

1. Nature and gravity of the accusation (Source 1 and Source 2)
2. Antecedents of the applicant (Source 1)
3. Whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant (Source 1)
4. Possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice (Source 1)

Additionally, the court may consider other factors such as evidence against the accused, background and ties to the community, criminal record, and probability of fleeing the jurisdiction (Source 2).

It is worth noting that the court has emphasized that personal liberty is paramount and anticipatory bail should not be denied merely on the gravity of the accusation (Source 1). 

A bail application for anticipatory bail in economic offenses can be filed by the accused person or any person on their behalf, such as their lawyer or family member, at various stages of the proceedings (Source 3).

The Supreme Court of India has consistently upheld the principles of natural justice in criminal law, emphasizing their importance in ensuring fairness and equity in the administration of justice. The principles of natural justice, as mentioned in Source 1, include 'audi alteram partem' (right to fair hearing) and 'nemo judex in causa sua' (no one should be a judge in his own cause).

In the context of criminal law, the Supreme Court has appellate jurisdiction as outlined in Article 134 of the Indian Constitution (Source 3). This article provides that an appeal shall lie to the Supreme Court from any judgment, final order, or sentence in a criminal proceeding of a High Court in the territory of India under certain conditions, such as when the High Court has reversed an order of acquittal and sentenced the accused to death, or when the High Court certifies that the case is a fit one for appeal to the Supreme Court under Article 134A.

The application of natural justice principles in criminal law is crucial to ensure that the accused is given a fair trial. The Supreme Court has emphasized the importance of these principles in various judgments, although specific cases are not mentioned in the provided context. The principles of natural justice are essential in preventing miscarriages of justice and upholding the rule of law.

In summary, the Supreme Court of India has the appellate jurisdiction to hear criminal matters as per Article 134 of the Indian Constitution and has consistently emphasized the importance of principles of natural justice in ensuring fairness and equity in the administration of criminal justice.

According to the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023, the burden of proof in criminal cases lies on the person who wishes the Court to believe in the existence of a particular fact. As stated in Section 106, "The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on that person who wishes the Court to believe in its existence, unless it is provided by any law that the proof of that fact shall lie on any particular person."

In a criminal case, the prosecution wishes the Court to believe in the guilt of the accused, and therefore, the burden of proof lies on the prosecution. As illustrated in Section 104, "A desires a Court to give judgment that B shall be punished for a crime which A says B has committed. A must prove that B has committed the crime."

This means that in a criminal trial, the prosecution must prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt. The accused is not required to prove their innocence, but rather, the prosecution must prove the existence of the facts that establish the accused's guilt.

This principle is a fundamental aspect of criminal jurisprudence and is essential to ensure that the accused receives a fair trial. The Supreme Court, in its appellate jurisdiction, would also apply this principle when reviewing criminal cases, as outlined in the case law provided.

SimpleRAG.answer:
The Supreme Court of India has interpreted the concept of 'Double Jeopardy' under Article 20(2) of the Indian Constitution. According to the Court, 'Double Jeopardy' is a constitutional safeguard that prohibits a person from being prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. 

In the case of 'State of Bombay v. S.L. Apte', the Supreme Court held that for double jeopardy to apply, the accused must be prosecuted and punished by a 'court' or 'judicial tribunal' in the first instance (Source 1). This means that the initial prosecution and punishment must be by a competent court or judicial tribunal for the protection against double jeopardy to be invoked.

Article 20(2) of the Indian Constitution states that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. This provision is a procedural defence that prevents an accused person from being tried again for the same charges following a valid acquittal or conviction (Source 2).

The Supreme Court's interpretation of Article 20(2) is aimed at safeguarding individuals against multiple punishments for the same offense, thereby upholding the principle of double jeopardy (Source 3). 

Therefore, the Supreme Court's stance on double jeopardy under Article 20(2) is that it protects individuals from being prosecuted and punished twice for the same offence, provided the initial prosecution and punishment were by a competent court or judicial tribunal.

According to the context provided, the protection against self-incrimination is explicitly mentioned in Article 20(3) of the Indian Constitution, which states that "No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself." This means that an individual cannot be forced to provide evidence or testimony that would implicate themselves in a criminal case.

As per the context, the relevant sections that support this protection are:

- Article 20(3) of the Indian Constitution, which explicitly states that no person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
- Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, which provides protection of life and personal liberty, stating that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.

There are no specific case laws mentioned in the context regarding compulsory self-incrimination. However, the provisions of Article 20(3) and Article 21 of the Indian Constitution provide a clear framework for protecting individuals against self-incrimination.

In summary, the Indian Constitution, through Article 20(3), provides a clear protection against self-incrimination, and no person accused of an offence can be compelled to testify against themselves.

1. **Deception of the complainant**: The prosecution must prove that the complainant was deceived.

3. **Delivery of property pursuant to such inducement**: The complainant must have delivered property (or something of value) as a result of the inducement.

The court held that anticipatory bail is a valuable safeguard for personal liberty and should not be denied merely on the gravity of the accusation. The guidelines are:

2. Examine the antecedents of the applicant.
3. Assess whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant.
4. Evaluate the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice.

It is also relevant to note that Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 (Source 2: bare_acts) and Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973 (Source 3: bare_acts) provide the statutory framework for anticipatory bail. These sections allow a person to apply for a direction for grant of bail if they have reason to believe that they may be arrested on an accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence.

In conclusion, the legal position on anticipatory bail for economic offences is that the court would consider the guidelines laid down by the Supreme Court, taking into account the nature and gravity of the offence, the applicant's antecedents, and the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice, while exercising its discretion to grant anticipatory bail under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 (or Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973, as applicable).

SimpleRAG.answer:
Based on the provided context, the differences between murder and culpable homicide can be identified from the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 Sections 100 and 101.

Culpable homicide, as defined in Section 100, is committed when an act is done with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that the act is likely to cause death.

On the other hand, murder, as defined in Section 101, is a specific type of culpable homicide that occurs under certain circumstances, such as:
(a) when the act is done with the intention of causing death;
(b) when the act is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause death;
(c) when the act is done with the intention of causing bodily injury and the injury intended is sufficient to cause death in the ordinary course of nature.

The key differences between murder and culpable homicide are:
1. Intent: Murder requires a more specific intent to cause death or knowledge that the act is likely to cause death, whereas culpable homicide can be committed with a lesser degree of intent or knowledge.
2. Severity: Murder is considered a more severe offense than culpable homicide, as it involves a greater degree of intent or recklessness.
3. Circumstances: Murder is culpable homicide that occurs under specific circumstances, as outlined in Section 101, whereas culpable homicide is a broader offense that encompasses a wider range of acts and intentions.

In summary, while all murder is culpable homicide, not all culpable homicide is murder. The distinction between the two offenses lies in the level of intent, severity, and circumstances surrounding the act. (Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, Sections 100 and 101)

According to Section 316 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, the ingredients of criminal breach of trust are:

1. The person must be entrusted with property or have dominion over property.
2. The person must dishonestly misappropriate or convert the property to their own use.
3. The person must use or dispose of the property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged.
4. The person must violate any legal contract, express or implied, which they have made touching the discharge of such trust.
5. The person must wilfully suffer any other person to misappropriate, convert, use, or dispose of the property.

These ingredients are essential to constitute the offense of criminal breach of trust, as defined in Section 316 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, which replaces Section 406 of the Indian Penal Code 1860.

Source 2 (case_law) provides what does not constitute an FIR, which indirectly helps in understanding what could be a valid FIR by exclusion:
- It should not be a report or statement recorded after the commencement of the investigation.

- It should not be merely an appeal for help without information about a cognizable offence.
- It should not be a complaint to the Magistrate or information given to the beat house, or over the phone to a police officer or Magistrate before a formal FIR is lodged.

Therefore, a valid FIR under the replaced CrPC (now BNSS Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023) is one that adheres to the conditions outlined in Section 173 of the BNSS 2023 and avoids the characteristics mentioned in Source 2 as not constituting an FIR.

Based on the provided context, the difference between bail and anticipatory bail can be understood as follows:

Bail, as discussed in Source 2, refers to the grant of bail to an accused who is already under arrest or in custody. The court may impose conditions on the accused to ensure their appearance in court.

Anticipatory bail, on the other hand, as discussed in Source 1, is a type of bail granted under Section 438 CrPC (now Section 483 BNSS) to a person who anticipates their arrest. The court grants anticipatory bail to protect the personal liberty of the individual, and the guidelines for granting anticipatory bail include considering the nature and gravity of the accusation, examining the antecedents of the applicant, assessing whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant, and evaluating the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice.

In essence, the key difference between bail and anticipatory bail is that bail is granted to an accused who is already in custody, whereas anticipatory bail is granted to a person who has not yet been arrested but anticipates their arrest. The considerations for granting bail, as outlined in Source 3, include factors like the nature and gravity of the alleged offense, evidence against the accused, and probability of fleeing the jurisdiction, which are also relevant for anticipatory bail, as mentioned in Source 1.

According to Section 120A of the Indian law, as mentioned in Source 1 (case_law) and Source 2 (bare_acts), the ingredients of criminal conspiracy are:

1. **Two or more persons**: There must be an agreement between at least two or more persons.
2. **Agreement with a common object**: The agreement must be with a common object to do, or cause to be done:
   - **An illegal act**; or
   - **An act which is not illegal by illegal means**.
3. **Act in pursuance of the agreement**: No agreement except an agreement to commit an offence shall amount to a criminal conspiracy unless **some act besides the agreement is done by one or more parties to such agreement in pursuance thereof**.

Additionally, as per the Explanation provided in Section 120A, **it is immaterial whether the illegal act is the ultimate object of such agreement or is merely incidental to that object**.

These are the essential ingredients of criminal conspiracy as per the provided context.

According to the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023, the legal requirements for electronic evidence admissibility are as follows:

1. **Admissibility**: Electronic or digital records are admissible as evidence, and their admissibility cannot be denied solely on the ground that they are electronic or digital records (Section 61, Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023).
2. **Same legal effect**: Electronic or digital records have the same legal effect, validity, and enforceability as other documents, subject to Section 63 (Section 61, Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023).
3. **Special provisions**: The contents of electronic records may be proved in accordance with the provisions of Section 63 (Section 62, Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023).

In summary, electronic evidence is admissible in court, has the same legal effect as other documents, and its contents can be proved in accordance with the provisions of Section 63 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023. 

Note: Although the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, is mentioned in Source 2, the specific provisions related to electronic evidence admissibility are outlined in the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023, as per the provided context.

SimpleRAG.answer:
The difference between examination-in-chief and cross-examination lies in their purpose, conduct, and the party conducting the examination.

Examination-in-chief is the first stage of questioning where the party who calls the witness leads questions to bring out the desired facts to establish its case (as per the Indian Evidence Act, 1872). The purpose is to introduce the witness, events, and documents relevant to the suit, and to clarify pleadings through the witness's account. Leading questions are permissible at this stage.

On the other hand, cross-examination is the questioning of a witness by the opposing party, governed by Section 137 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872. The objective of cross-examination is to test the veracity and reliability of the witness's testimony presented during examination-in-chief, by bringing out contradictions, casting doubts, or weakening the case. Leading questions can be put to challenge the witness's memory, facts stated, and credibility.

In summary, examination-in-chief is conducted by the party calling the witness to establish its case, while cross-examination is conducted by the opposing party to challenge and test the witness's testimony.

2. Fraudulent or dishonest inducement, and

Moreover, Source 2 specifically mentions that there must be clear evidence of "mens rea" (guilty intention) from the inception of the transaction. This suggests that the dishonest intention must be deliberate and present from the start, rather than a consequence of subsequent actions or failures to fulfill contractual obligations.

While Source 3 introduces the term 'Dishonestly' under Section 24 without providing detailed information, the concept of dishonest intention as discussed in the context of cheating under Section 420 IPC emphasizes the necessity of proving a deliberate and fraudulent intent from the beginning of the transaction.

In summary, dishonest intention under the IPC, particularly in the context of Section 420 (cheating), is constituted by a deliberate and fraudulent intent to deceive, which must be present from the inception of the transaction, and is characterized by elements such as deception, dishonest inducement, and the delivery of property based on such inducement.

Based on the provided context, the difference between theft, robbery, and dacoity can be explained as follows:

1. **Theft**: The context does not explicitly define theft, but it is mentioned in Section 390 as a component of robbery. It can be inferred that theft refers to the act of taking someone's property without their consent.

2. **Robbery**: As defined in Section 390, robbery involves either theft or extortion. Specifically, theft is considered robbery if, during the commission or attempt to commit theft, the offender causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint to any person.

3. **Dacoity**: As per Section 391, dacoity occurs when five or more persons jointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery. Alternatively, if the total number of persons committing or attempting to commit a robbery, along with those aiding in the act, amounts to five or more, it is considered dacoity.

In summary, the key differences are:
- **Theft** is the basic act of taking someone's property without consent.
- **Robbery** involves theft or extortion with additional elements of violence, fear, or intimidation.
- **Dacoity** is a more severe form of robbery involving five or more persons, either as direct participants or as aiders in the act.

In summary, wrongful restraint is about preventing someone from moving in a particular direction, whereas wrongful confinement is about restricting someone's liberty by confining them to a specific area.

Based on the provided context, the difference between rioting and unlawful assembly can be understood through the sections mentioned.

An unlawful assembly is a gathering of people with a common object that is unlawful. However, it becomes rioting when force or violence is used by the unlawful assembly or any member thereof in prosecution of their common object, as stated in Section 146. This means that every member of the unlawful assembly can be held guilty of rioting if they use force or violence.

In contrast, an unlawful assembly itself does not necessarily involve the use of force or violence but is still an offense. The owner or occupier of the land where an unlawful assembly takes place can be held liable under Section 154 if they fail to give notice to the authorities or prevent the assembly.

Section 156 further clarifies the liability of agents or managers of landowners or occupiers if a riot is committed for their benefit. This suggests that the distinction between rioting and unlawful assembly lies in the use of force or violence. Unlawful assembly is a broader term that encompasses any gathering with an unlawful purpose, while rioting is a more specific offense that involves the use of force or violence by an unlawful assembly.

In summary, the key difference between rioting and unlawful assembly is the use of force or violence. An unlawful assembly can exist without rioting, but once force or violence is used, it becomes rioting, and all members of the assembly can be held liable under Section 146.

According to the provided context, the legal requirements for arrest without warrant are as follows:

Under the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973, Section 41, a police officer may arrest without a warrant if:
- The person has been concerned in any cognizable offence.
- A reasonable complaint has been made against the person.
- Credible information has been received about the person's involvement in a cognizable offence.
- A reasonable suspicion exists about the person's involvement in a cognizable offence.

Under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, Section 35, and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023, Section 35, a police officer may arrest without a warrant if:
- The person commits a cognizable offence in the presence of a police officer (Section 35(1)(a)).
- A reasonable complaint has been made, or credible information has been received, or a reasonable suspicion exists that the person has committed a cognizable offence punishable with imprisonment for a term which may be less than seven years or which may extend to seven years, whether with or without fine, provided certain conditions are satisfied (Section 35(1)(b)).

Note: The conditions mentioned in Section 35(1)(b) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 are not fully specified in the provided context, as the text is truncated. However, the general requirements for arrest without a warrant are outlined above.

According to the provided context, a continuing offence is defined under Section 518 of both the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023. 

In the case of a continuing offence, as per Section 518, a fresh period of limitation shall begin to run at every moment of the time during which the offence continues. This implies that the limitation period for a continuing offence restarts at every instance the offence is ongoing, rather than being calculated from the initial commission of the offence.

This interpretation is similar to the provision under Section 472 of the Old CrPC, as referenced in the context. 

Therefore, a continuing offence is one where the act of commission is ongoing, and the limitation period for prosecution restarts at every moment the offence continues.

The doctrine of common intention under Section 34 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) is explained in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, which replaces the IPC. According to Section 3 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, when a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

In other words, Section 34 (now replaced by Section 3 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023) implies that if multiple individuals participate in a crime with a shared intention, each person can be held liable for the entire act, as if they had committed it individually. This section emphasizes the concept of collective responsibility for crimes committed in pursuit of a common goal or intention.

Reference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, Section 3 (Replacing IPC Section 34).

The ingredients of forgery can be identified from the provided context, specifically from Section 336 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and the explanation of Section 463 from the Old IPC. 

To constitute forgery, the following ingredients must be present:
1. Making of a false document or false electronic record: This involves the creation or alteration of a document or electronic record with the intention of deceiving.
2. Intent to cause damage or injury: The person must intend to cause harm or injury to the public or to any person through the false document or record.
3. Intent to support any claim or title: The false document or record must be made to support a false claim or title.
4. Intent to cause any person to part with property: The forgery must be intended to deceive someone into giving up their property.
5. Intent to enter into any express or implied contract: The false document or record must be made with the intention of inducing someone to enter into a contract.
6. Intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed: The person must have the intention of committing fraud or facilitating fraud through the false document or record.

These ingredients are outlined in Section 336(1) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, which is similar to Section 463 of the Old IPC, as mentioned in the provided context.

According to the context, specifically Section 441 of the Indian Penal Code (as mentioned in Source 1) and Section 329(1) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 (as mentioned in Source 3), criminal trespass is constituted when:

1. A person enters into or upon property in the possession of another with the intent to:
   - Commit an offence, or
   - Intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of such property.

2. A person, having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with the intent to:
   - Intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of the property, or
   - Commit an offence.

In essence, criminal trespass involves entering someone else's property with the intention of committing a crime or causing disturbance, or staying on the property with such intentions after being allowed to enter.

According to the context provided, specifically Source 1 (case_law), the legal grounds for seeking anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC (now Section 483 BNSS) are based on the following guidelines laid down by the Supreme Court:

1. **The nature and gravity of the accusation**: The court considers the seriousness of the charges made against the applicant.
2. **The antecedents of the applicant**: The court examines the applicant's past behavior and criminal record.
3. **Whether the accusation is made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant**: The court assesses if the accusation is motivated by malice or an intent to harm the applicant's reputation.
4. **The possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice**: The court evaluates the likelihood of the applicant evading the legal process.

These guidelines emphasize that anticipatory bail should not be denied solely based on the gravity of the accusation, and that personal liberty is paramount. The court's decision is guided by these factors to determine whether anticipatory bail should be granted.

According to the provided context, valid service of summons can be constituted in the following ways:

1. **Personal Service**: As per Section 64 (not explicitly mentioned but referred to in Sections 67 of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023), service can be effected by delivering or tendering the summons to the person summoned.

2. **Service on Government Servant**: Under Section 68 of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, where the person summoned is a government servant, the summons is sent to the head of their office, who then causes the summons to be served. The head's signature on the returned summons serves as evidence of due service.

3. **Affixing to Conspicuous Part of House/Homestead**: If service cannot be effected through the above methods, Section 67 of both Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 provides that the serving officer can affix a duplicate of the summons to a conspicuous part of the house or homestead where the person summoned ordinarily resides. The Court may then, after making inquiries, declare the summons duly served or order fresh service.

In summary, valid service of summons can be achieved through personal service, service through the head of the office for government servants, or by affixing the summons to the person's residence if other methods fail, as per the provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023.

It is also worth noting that the context mentions the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000, which has been repealed and replaced by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015. According to the provided context, the legal requirements for compounding of offenses in Indian law are as follows:

1. **Eligibility**: Compounding of an offense is a mechanism whereby the defaulter can escape prosecution by paying a sum of money. This is available for specified offenses that can be compounded by the competent authority either before or after the initiation of proceedings (Source 1).

2. **Category B Offenses**: For Category B offenses, compounding is not allowed if it is not a 'First Offence'. A 'First Offence' means an offense that was committed prior to the issue of any letter or notice related to prosecution, intimation of filing a prosecution complaint, or the launch of prosecution. It also includes offenses voluntarily disclosed by the assessee with the filing of a compounding application (Source 2).

3. **Category A Offenses**: Category A offenses cannot be compounded if an application for compounding has already been made more than three times in respect of such offenses. However, in exceptional circumstances, the Principal CCIT of the concerned region may approve compounding requested in more than three occasions (Source 3).

4. **Multiple Applications**: If an assessee files multiple applications for one or more assessment years in one instance, all these applications shall be treated as one occasion for the purpose of determining the number of times compounding has been requested (Source 3).

In summary, the legal requirements for compounding of offenses in Indian law involve determining the category of the offense, checking if it is a 'First Offence' for Category B offenses, and ensuring that the application for compounding is within the allowed limits for Category A offenses.

SimpleRAG.answer:
Under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963, the following contracts are not specifically enforceable:

1. A contract for the non-performance of which compensation in money is an adequate relief (Section 14(1)(a)).
2. A contract which runs into such minute or numerous details or which is so dependent on the personal qualifications or volition of the parties, or otherwise from its nature is such, that the court cannot enforce specific performance of its material terms (Section 14(1)(b)).
3. A contract which is in its nature determinable (Section 14(1)(c)).
4. A contract the performance of which involves the performance of a continuous duty (Section 14(1)(d)).

These contracts are explicitly excluded from specific enforcement under the Act.

Under Section 31 of the Specific Relief Act 1963, a written instrument can be cancelled when:

1. The instrument is void or voidable.
2. The person against whom the instrument is void or voidable has a reasonable apprehension that the instrument, if left outstanding, may cause them serious injury.

In such cases, the court may, in its discretion, adjudge the instrument void or voidable and order it to be delivered up and cancelled. Additionally, if the instrument has been registered under the Indian Registration Act, 1908, the court shall send a copy of its decree to the registration officer, who shall note the cancellation. (Section 31, Sub-sections 1 and 2)

SimpleRAG.answer:
The context provided does not mention the doctrine of lis pendens under Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act. However, I can provide general information about the doctrine of lis pendens.

The doctrine of lis pendens is a legal principle that means "a suit pending." It is embodied in Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, which states that during the pendency of a suit, a property cannot be transferred or alienated by any party to the suit. This means that if a suit is pending in a court regarding a particular property, any transfer or alienation of that property during the pendency of the suit shall be voidable at the option of the purchaser.

In other words, if a person purchases a property that is the subject of a pending lawsuit, the purchase may be set aside if the lawsuit ultimately results in a judgment against the seller. The doctrine of lis pendens is intended to prevent parties to a lawsuit from transferring property in a way that could affect the outcome of the case or the rights of the parties involved.

Since the context does not provide any specific information about the doctrine of lis pendens, I have provided general information about the principle. If you have any further questions or need more specific information, please let me know.

Based on the context provided, the court's duty under Section 3 of the Limitation Act when a suit is time-barred is not explicitly stated in the given sources. However, according to general principles of the Limitation Act, when a suit is time-barred, the court is duty-bound to dismiss the suit if it is filed beyond the prescribed period of limitation, as the court has no discretion to condone the delay in such cases.

Section 3 of the Limitation Act generally states that a suit, appeal, or application filed after the prescribed period of limitation is barred. The court's primary duty in such cases is to apply the provisions of the Limitation Act and dismiss the suit if it is time-barred, unless the delay can be condoned under Section 5 of the Limitation Act, which allows for condonation of delay if the applicant can show sufficient cause for the delay. 

In the context of Section 5, as mentioned in Source 3, the court has the discretion to condone the delay if it finds sufficient cause, and the 2002 amendment is said to have taken a liberal approach in this regard. However, this does not directly answer the question regarding Section 3 but highlights the court's role in managing time-barred suits.

The context provided does not mention Section 14 of the Limitation Act. However, it does mention Section 516 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, which deals with the exclusion of time in certain cases, and also provides information about the exclusion of time under Section 149 and Section 148 of the Income Tax Act.

According to the context, for exclusion of time under Section 149 for the issue of notice under Section 148A(b), the following conditions must be met:
i. The information received by the AO emanates from a statement recorded or documents impounded under summons or survey, as the case may be, on or before 31st March of the financial year; and
ii. It should be in consequence of a search initiated, the last warrant executed, or requisition made after 15th March of that financial year.

Additionally, Section 516 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 provides that in computing the period of limitation, the time during which any person has been prosecuting with due diligence another prosecution, whether in a Court of first instance or in a Court of appeal or revision, against the offender, shall be excluded, provided that:
- The prosecution relates to the same facts, and
- It is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which from defect of jurisdiction or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it.

It is also mentioned that to compute the period of limitation for issuance of notice under new Section 148, the time or extended time allowed to the assessee in providing an opportunity of being heard or period during which such proceedings before issuance of notice under Section 148 are stayed by an order or injunction of any court, shall be excluded. If after excluding such period, time available to the Assessing Officer for passing order, about fitness of a case for issue of Section 148 notice, is 7 days or less, the remaining time shall be extended to 7 days.

3. The agreement should be to submit to arbitration all or certain disputes.

As per Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, the appointment of arbitrators can be handled in the following manner:
- The parties are free to agree on a procedure for appointing the arbitrator or arbitrators.

- If a party fails to appoint an arbitrator within thirty days from the date of receipt of a request to do so, or if the two appointed arbitrators fail to reach an agreement on the appointment of the third arbitrator within thirty days from the date of their appointment, the appointment shall be made by the Supreme Court or the High Court, as the case may be.

This implies that an arbitral award becomes enforceable under Section 36 once the time limit for challenging the award under Section 34 has expired, unless a stay has been granted by the court. 

In other words, an arbitral award becomes enforceable under Section 36 after the expiry of the 3-month period (or such other period as may be specified) for filing an application to set aside the award under Section 34, or if such an application has been filed, upon the dismissal of the application, unless a stay has been granted by the court.

3. Alternatively, the complaint can be made by a person authorized by the Central Government.

According to Section 3 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act 2012, the following acts amount to penetrative sexual assault:

1. A person penetrates their penis, to any extent, into the vagina, mouth, urethra, or anus of a child or makes the child do so with them or any other person (Section 3(a)).
2. A person inserts, to any extent, any object or a part of the body (not being the penis) into the vagina, urethra, or anus of the child or makes the child do so with them or any other person (Section 3(b)).
3. A person manipulates any part of the body of the child so as to cause penetration into the vagina, urethra, anus, or any part of the body of the child or makes the child do so (Section 3(c)).

These acts constitute penetrative sexual assault under the POCSO Act.

SimpleRAG.answer:
Under Section 24 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act 2012 (POCSO), the police must record the statement of a child in the following manner:

1. **At a suitable location**: The statement of the child shall be recorded at the residence of the child or at a place where he usually resides or at the place of his choice.
2. **By a woman police officer**: The statement shall be recorded by a woman police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector, as far as practicable.
3. **In plain clothes**: The police officer while recording the statement of the child shall not be in uniform.
4. **No contact with the accused**: The police officer making the investigation shall ensure that at no point of time the child comes into contact in any way with the accused.
5. **No detention at police station**: No child shall be detained in the police station.

These provisions aim to ensure that the child feels safe and comfortable while giving their statement, and that their rights are protected during the investigation process.

SimpleRAG.answer:
According to Section 33 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, the Special Court powers and child safeguards are as follows:

1. **Taking Cognizance**: A Special Court may take cognizance of any offence without the accused being committed to it for trial, upon receiving a complaint of facts which constitute such offence, or upon a police report of such facts (Section 33(1)).

2. **Recording Examination**: The Special Public Prosecutor, or the counsel appearing for the accused, shall communicate the questions to be put to the child to the Special Court, which shall in turn put those questions to the child, while recording the examination-in-chief, cross-examination, or re-examination of the child (Section 33(2)).

3. **Additional Powers**: The Special Court may exercise additional powers as specified in Section 33(3), although the specific powers are not detailed in the provided context.

These provisions aim to ensure a safe and sensitive environment for children during the trial process, while also enabling the Special Court to effectively try offences under the POCSO Act.
